T1566.name: Phishing
T1566.description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems. All forms of phishing are electronically delivered social engineering.
T1566.detection: Network intrusion detection systems and email gateways can be used to detect phishing with malicious attachments in transit.

T1566.001.name: Spear Phishing Attachment
T1566.001.description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.
T1566.001.detection: Anti-virus can potentially detect malicious documents through signatures.

T1566.002.name: Spear Phishing Link
T1566.002.description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.
T1566.002.detection: URL inspection within email, network traffic analysis.

T1190.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
T1190.description: Adversaries may attempt to exploit a weakness in an Internet-facing host or system to initially access a network.
T1190.detection: Monitor application logs for abnormal behavior that may indicate attempted or successful exploitation.

T1059.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
T1059.description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
T1059.detection: Monitor for suspicious process execution from interpreter programs.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell
T1059.001.description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.
T1059.001.detection: Enable PowerShell logging (module, script block, transcription).

T1059.003.name: Windows Command Shell
T1059.003.description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows command shell for execution.

T1547.001.name: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
T1547.001.description: Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key.
T1547.001.detection: Monitor Registry for changes to run keys.

T1068.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
T1068.description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in an attempt to elevate privileges.
T1068.detection: Monitor for exploitation indicators using intrusion detection systems.

T1003.name: OS Credential Dumping
T1003.description: Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material.
T1003.detection: Monitor for unexpected processes accessing LSASS memory.

T1021.name: Remote Services
T1021.description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to log into a service specifically designed to accept remote connections.
T1021.detection: Monitor for user accounts logging into services via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) or SSH.

T1041.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
T1041.description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.
T1041.detection: Analyze network data for uncommon data flows.

T1486.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
T1486.description: Adversaries may encrypt data on target systems or on large numbers of systems in a network to interrupt availability to system and network resources.
T1486.detection: Monitor for large amounts of file modifications.

T1071.name: Application Layer Protocol
T1071.description: Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols to avoid detection/network filtering.
T1071.detection: Analyze network data for uncommon data flows using application layer protocols.

T1082.name: System Information Discovery
T1082.description: An adversary may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware.
T1082.detection: Monitor processes and command-line arguments for actions that could be taken to gather system and network information.

T1055.name: Process Injection
T1055.description: Adversaries may inject code into processes in order to evade process-based defenses as well as possibly elevate privileges.
T1055.detection: Monitor for processes injecting code into other processes.
